author: moritajavier239-rgb
body: https://github.com/moritajavier239-rgb/ollama-js/actions/workflows/publish.yaml

title: gpt-oss:120b intermittent mid-response degeneration (ellipsis/junk token walls, then recovery) on Apple Silicon — persists across KV f16, FA off, 0.31.2; MLX engine control shows clean output
author: DarkNebulaAI
body:
### What is the issue?

gpt-oss:120b intermittently degenerates into walls of ellipsis/junk-whitespace tokens mid-response, then recovers within the same response. It happens roughly 2-6 times/day under an agentic workload and we have a cross-engine control showing the model itself is fine.

**Symptom** (delivered via /v1/chat/completions, streamed): the visible content contains dozens of lines like:

```
I've ...
...
 ...
......
The
...
We'll......
Here...
</analysis<|message|>The assistant is repeatedly giving nonsense output after placing call. Need to correct. ...
The call is still in progress; I'll let you know the weekend plans as soon as I have the answer.
```

Note the trailing recovery: after hundreds of junk tokens the model produces the correct final sentence. Occasionally raw harmony markers (`</analysis`, `<|message|>`) and analysis-channel prose leak into `content` during these episodes.

### Environment

- Mac Studio-class Apple Silicon (M3 Max, 128 GB), macOS (Metal)
- ollama 0.31.1 and 0.31.2 (server), model `gpt-oss:120b` (MXFP4), `num_ctx` 65536–81920
- Client: OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions`, streaming, ~25–50 tools in the request, prompts 10–25k tokens (agent framework)

### What we ruled out (each eliminated, bug persisted)

- `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0` → **f16** (q8 KV made it much worse/deterministic; f16 reduced frequency but episodes continued)
- ollama 0.31.1 → **0.31.2** (engine update)
- Explicit sampling: `temperature 1.0, top_p 1.0, repeat_penalty 1.0`
- `OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1` → **0** (episodes persisted with FA off)
- Client max_tokens raised 4096 → 16384 (rules out truncation/continuation artifacts)
- Context shift: num_ctx raised to 81920 with prompts ≤64k (shift can never trigger); episodes persisted

### Reproduction profile

Hard to trigger synthetically: clean at 6k/24k/48k/65k-token prompts in isolated tests, clean over repeated multi-turn tool-call round-trips with 20+ tool schemas. In production it fires on the *first assistant message following a tool result* inside long-lived agent conversations (10-25k tokens, many tools). Intermittent — same request shape usually works.

### Cross-engine control (why we believe this is the llama.cpp/Metal path in ollama)

We served the equivalent weights (`mlx-community/gpt-oss-120b-MXFP4-Q4`) via LM Studio's MLX engine behind the same agent framework, same prompts, same tools, for a comparison window: **zero degeneration episodes** across all turns, including the long post-tool-result turns that trigger it under ollama. Same model, same workload — only the inference engine changed.

Possibly related: #12403 (garbage output since 0.11.11/0.12.0), #11982 (nonsense output with OLLAMA_NEW_ESTIMATES), #12113 (FA NaN panic) — but unlike #12403 this is intermittent mid-response degeneration with recovery, not persistent garbage.

Happy to provide full request/response captures or run instrumented builds.

### OS

macOS

### GPU

Apple

### CPU

Apple

### Ollama version

0.31.2

title: MLX runner startup timeout ignores OLLAMA_LOAD_TIMEOUT and can kill a model just before it becomes ready
author: burtl
body:
### What happened?

Hey — I ran into an MLX startup timeout that looks like the runner is being killed a little too early.

With `gemma4:12b-mlx`, Ollama starts the MLX subprocess, initializes MLX, loads the main model tensors, and then loads the draft model. After that, stock Ollama waits until exactly two minutes and returns:

```text
timeout waiting for mlx runner to start
```

I reproduced this on both Ollama `0.31.1` and `0.31.2`.

The relevant startup sequence in the logs is:

```text
Gemma4UnifiedForConditionalGeneration
loaded 1389 tensors
Gemma4UnifiedAssistantForCausalLM
Loaded draft model tensor_prefix=draft. config=draft/config.json arrays=49
```

Then the parent process times out and stops the runner.

I traced this to `x/mlxrunner/client.go`, where `Client.WaitUntilRunning` has a fixed timeout:

```go
timeout := time.After(2 * time.Minute)
```

That path polls `/v1/status`, but it does not appear to honor `OLLAMA_LOAD_TIMEOUT`. Setting `OLLAMA_LOAD_TIMEOUT=0` did not change the two-minute cutoff.

### Test with a small patch

I built an isolated diagnostic runtime from official Ollama `v0.31.2` at commit:

```text
a6293eb5164214f15e7856268f3adddeae1026e2
```

The only source change was:

```diff
- timeout := time.After(2 * time.Minute)
+ timeout := time.After(envconfig.LoadTimeout())
```

Using the matching official MLX runtime payload and:

```text
OLLAMA_LOAD_TIMEOUT=15m
```

the same runner became ready in **137.008 seconds** and returned HTTP 200.

That is **17.008 seconds after** the stock two-minute deadline.

I also have an older successful startup of the same model on the same machine at **109.104 seconds**, so startup time seems to vary around the current hard cutoff.

### Expected behavior

It would be helpful if the MLX runner startup wait either:

- honored `OLLAMA_LOAD_TIMEOUT`, or
- had its own configurable timeout.

I’m not claiming this explains why the final part of MLX startup sometimes takes longer. This only shows that the fixed two-minute deadline can terminate a runner that is still progressing and becomes healthy shortly afterward.

### Environment

- Mac mini `Mac14,3`
- Apple M2
- 16 GB unified memory
- macOS 26.5.1 (`25F80`)
- Ollama `0.31.1` and `0.31.2`
- Model: `gemma4:12b-mlx`
- Model digest: `117d0d84cf2ab865feb59afc2cd30ff5d55f0035e05eb8d1b814f9688e3f3671`
- Model architecture: `gemma4_unified`
- Quantization: `nvfp4`
- Model store is on an external Thunderbolt SSD

I can provide the full debug logs, timing comparison, patched binary checksum, and exact test commands if useful.

title: FAQ's description of the update check omits the per-install identifier it sends
author: kobihikri
body:
Hi — a small documentation accuracy point about the update check.

The FAQ says (`docs/faq.mdx`):

> Ollama runs locally. We don't see your prompts or data when you run locally.

That's true for prompts and data. But the desktop update check sends a bit more than the FAQ implies, and I think it's worth naming so users can make an informed choice.

In `app/updater/updater.go`, the app requests `https://ollama.com/api/update` hourly, and the request is signed — `auth.Sign` in `auth/auth.go` reads `~/.ollama/id_ed25519` and sets the `Authorization` header to `<public-key>:<signature>`. So each check carries the install's **ed25519 public key**, which is a stable, unique per-installation identifier, alongside the OS/arch/version already needed for the update itself.

I'm not suggesting the update check should change — I saw #4498, and I understand it's intrinsic to the desktop app (and that a standalone binary without it exists). This is only about the docs: a reader who wants to know what leaves their machine would currently not learn that a persistent identifier is part of the update ping.

Two small things that might help, either or both:

1. In `faq.mdx`, briefly note that the desktop app checks `ollama.com/api/update` and that the request is signed with the installation's key (so it includes a stable identifier).
2. Optionally, have the updater honour the existing `disable_ollama_cloud` setting — right now that setting isn't referenced in `app/updater/` (`grep -rn disable_ollama_cloud app/updater/` → nothing), so a user who sets it may reasonably expect it to cover this and it doesn't.

Happy to open a PR for the doc wording if that's welcome. (Separately, release signing / provenance is already tracked in #9132 and attribution in #3185 — not re-raising those here.)

*(Checked at `82f905cd9c06c6f0254d74c5326aa2a7f2f07e1f`. Written with AI assistance.)*

title: Gemma4 vision cannot recognize text in high-resolution images due to hardcoded max_soft_tokens=280
author: LifetimeVip
body:
### What is the issue?

Gemma4 vision model fails to recognize text in high-resolution images (screenshots, document photos). The image has sufficient resolution and clarity, but recognition fails because Ollama hardcodes the image token budget (max_soft_tokens) to 280 in model/models/gemma4/process_image.go, causing aggressive downscaling. For example, a 1920x1080 screenshot is compressed to ~1056x576. Additionally, Bilinear interpolation is used instead of Bicubic, further degrading text. The same model works correctly with llama.cpp --image-max-tokens 1120. Expected: configurable token budget or higher default.

### Relevant log output

No error logs produced. Model loads and responds normally but generates incorrect text recognition due to image quality loss in preprocessing pipeline.

### OS

Windows

### GPU

Nvidia

### CPU

Intel

### Ollama version

0.31.2

title: [Bug] Ollama hardcodes Gemma 4 image token budget (max_soft_tokens=280), breaking OCR on high-resolution images
author: LifetimeVip
body:
### What is the issue?

**What happened:**
Gemma 4 vision model (ollama pull gemma4) fails to recognize text in high-resolution images. When asked to identify text in a 1920x1080 screenshot containing clear text, the model responds with "There is no discernible text in this image" or similar false negatives. This is reproducible via both CLI and REST API.

The root cause is that Ollama silently downscales images before sending them to the model ? there is no user consent and no way to control or disable this behavior.

**What did you expect to happen:**
The image should be passed to the model at its original resolution. If downscaling is necessary, it should be opt-in and configurable. Currently Ollama offers zero control over image preprocessing.

**Root cause:**
Ollama hardcodes max_soft_tokens to 280 in model/models/gemma4/process_image.go. This forces images to approximately 645K pixels after pooling. A 1920x1080 screenshot is silently downscaled to ~1056x576 (31% of original area). A 4K image is reduced to ~8%. Bilinear interpolation (draw.BiLinear.Scale) is used for resizing, further softening text edges compared to Bicubic used by reference implementations.

There is no justification for this ? the model supports configurable token budgets (70/140/280/560/1120) and can handle high-resolution input natively. Ollama should not destroy image quality without user knowledge or consent.

**Evidence this is an Ollama issue, not a model issue:**
- Same model + same image works correctly with llama.cpp: --image-max-tokens 1120
- Same model + same image works correctly with HuggingFace Transformers: max_soft_tokens=1120
- Feature request #15626 already filed asking to expose max_soft_tokens

Related to #15626. This report adds: evidence that Bilinear interpolation further degrades text, concrete reproduction steps, and the argument that the default should be no downscaling at all. Note: raising maxTokens to 1120 may cause OOM on some configs (see #15626) ? this should be handled gracefully, not by silently degrading quality for everyone.

**Reproduction (CLI):**
```
ollama pull gemma4
ollama run gemma4 "Read all text in this image verbatim" C:\test\screenshot.png
```

Expected: model reads the visible text correctly.
Actual: model claims no text is present or hallucinates content.

### Relevant log output

```shell
time=2026-07-12T20:19:46.418-07:00 level=INFO source=sched.go:1158 msg="disabling mmap for llama-server load by default"
load_tensors: offloaded 41/41 layers to GPU
llama_context: n_ctx         = 4096
llama_context: n_ctx_seq (4096) < n_ctx_train (262144) -- the full capacity of the model will not be utilized

# Model response for a 1920x1080 screenshot with clear, readable text:
"There is no discernible text in the image. The image appears to be an abstract or textured background pattern, but it does not contain any readable letters, words, or numbers."

# Same image with llama.cpp --image-max-tokens 1120:
[correctly outputs all visible text]
```

### OS

Windows 11 Pro (Build 26200)

### GPU

Nvidia GeForce RTX 3080 Ti Laptop GPU (16GB VRAM)

### CPU

Intel 12th Gen Core i7-12800HX

### Ollama version

0.31.2

title: convert: malformed safetensors header can panic model conversion
author: nafey7
body:
### What happened

When converting a model from safetensors (e.g. `ollama create` / `POST /api/create` referencing an uploaded blob), the safetensors header is parsed in `convert/reader_safetensors.go` without the bounds checks the GGUF readers apply. A malformed header causes a `panic` rather than a clean error.

Three fields read directly from the file are used unchecked:

1. **Header length prefix** — the leading 8-byte `int64` length is passed straight to `make([]byte, 0, n)`. A negative or very large value causes a `makeslice` panic / oversized allocation before any data is read.
2. **`data_offsets`** — indexed as `Offsets[0]` / `Offsets[1]` with no length check. An empty or single-element array panics with `index out of range`.
3. **Inverted / out-of-range offsets** — `Offsets[1] < Offsets[0]` yields a negative tensor `size`, which panics in the `make([]T, size/n)` calls in `WriteTo`.

Because conversion runs in a goroutine without `recover`, the panic isn't contained to the request.

### Impact

A crafted (or corrupted) safetensors file passed through conversion crashes the process instead of returning an error. This is a robustness/availability issue on the model-import path.

### Expected

Malformed safetensors input should be rejected with an error, consistent with how the GGUF readers (`fs/ggml`, `fs/gguf`) bound every length, count, and offset (`checkedLength`, `MaxArraySize`, file-size checks).

### Fix

PR incoming: validate the header length against the file size and a fixed cap, and validate that each tensor's `data_offsets` has exactly two non-negative, non-inverted entries within the data section, before use. Includes regression tests.

### Environment

- Affected file: `convert/reader_safetensors.go`
- Reproducible on current `main`.

title: GLM 5.2 supports max thinking-effort but ollama lunch pi --config does not expose thinkingLevelMap
author: Q-Peppa
body:
**Description:**

When launching GLM 5.2 via `ollama lunch pi --config`, the generated configuration does not include a `thinkingLevelMap` field. This prevents users from utilising the model’s maximum thinking effort capability, even though the model itself supports it.

**Current behaviour**

The default configuration produced by `ollama lunch pi --config` for `glm-5.2:cloud` looks like this:

```json
{
  "_launch": true,
  "contextWindow": 1000000,
  "id": "glm-5.2:cloud",
  "input": ["text"],
  "reasoning": true
}
```

There is no `thinkingLevelMap`, so the thinking effort cannot be set to `max`.

**Expected behaviour**

The default configuration should include a `thinkingLevelMap` that maps the highest thinking levels to `"max"`, allowing the model to use its full reasoning capacity out of the box. The desired default config is:

```json
{
  "_launch": true,
  "contextWindow": 1000000,
  "id": "glm-5.2:cloud",
  "input": ["text"],
  "reasoning": true,
  "thinkingLevelMap": {
    "xhigh": "max",
    "max": "max"
  }
}
```

**Why this matters**

- GLM 5.2 supports a `max` thinking effort, which is essential for complex reasoning tasks.
- Without `thinkingLevelMap`, users must manually edit the config after every launch, which is error-prone and inconvenient.
- Adding this mapping to the default config would align the tool’s behaviour with the model’s capabilities and improve the out-of-the-box experience.

**Proposed solution**

Update the default configuration template for `glm-5.2:cloud` to include the `thinkingLevelMap` as shown above. If the mapping is model-specific, consider adding a mechanism to detect or specify the model’s supported thinking levels automatically.

**Additional context**

- `ollama lunch pi --config` is used to generate launch configurations.
- The issue was observed with GLM 5.2, but other models that support `max` thinking effort may also be affected.
- https://docs.z.ai/guides/capabilities/thinking

title: Tool-parameter JSON-Schema constraints (minimum/maximum/default/…) silently dropped at parse time
author: laurigates
body:
### What is happening

JSON-Schema validation keywords on tool parameters are silently discarded when a `/api/chat` request is parsed, so they never reach the model. `type`, `description`, `enum`, `anyOf`, `items`, `properties`, and `required` survive; everything else — `minimum`, `maximum`, `exclusiveMinimum`, `exclusiveMaximum`, `multipleOf`, `default`, `format`, `pattern`, `minLength`/`maxLength`, `minItems`/`maxItems`, `const` — is dropped.

### Root cause

The drop is at unmarshal time, before any chat template runs. `api.ToolProperty` (`api/types.go`) has fields only for `AnyOf`, `Type`, `Items`, `Description`, `Enum`, `Properties`, `Required`:

```go
type ToolProperty struct {
	AnyOf       []ToolProperty     `json:"anyOf,omitempty"`
	Type        PropertyType       `json:"type,omitempty"`
	Items       any                `json:"items,omitempty"`
	Description string             `json:"description,omitempty"`
	Enum        []any              `json:"enum,omitempty"`
	Properties  *ToolPropertiesMap `json:"properties,omitempty"`
	Required    []string           `json:"required,omitempty"`
}
```

`json.Unmarshal` drops any key without a matching struct field, so a keyword like `minimum` on an incoming tool param is gone before the model template is ever invoked. This is template-independent — it affects every model. `ToolFunctionParameters` similarly has no place for these keywords.

### Reproduction (Ollama 0.31.1, and reproduces on current `main`)

Send a fully-constrained tool via native `/api/chat` and ask the model to echo back the schema it received:

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/chat -d '{
  "model":"gemma4:12b","stream":false,"think":true,
  "messages":[
    {"role":"system","content":"You are a precise tool-use assistant."},
    {"role":"user","content":"Do NOT call any tool. For search_records, output a JSON object mapping each parameter to EXACTLY the JSON-schema keywords you were given (type, description, minimum, maximum, default, exclusiveMinimum, multipleOf, enum, anyOf — only the ones present). Report only what you received; do not invent."}
  ],
  "tools":[{"type":"function","function":{"name":"search_records","description":"Search records.","parameters":{"type":"object","properties":{
    "page_limit":{"type":"integer","description":"Results per page.","minimum":1,"maximum":100,"default":20},
    "threshold":{"type":"number","description":"Similarity cutoff.","minimum":0.0,"maximum":1.0,"exclusiveMinimum":0.0,"multipleOf":0.01},
    "mode":{"type":"string","description":"Search mode.","enum":["fuzzy","exact"]},
    "tags":{"anyOf":[{"type":"string"},{"type":"array"}],"description":"Tag filter."}
  },"required":["page_limit"]}}}]
}'
```

The model returns `type` + `description` only for `page_limit` and `threshold`, `enum` for `mode`, and a `['string','array']` union for `tags` — i.e. exactly the struct's field set. The numeric bounds and `default` are absent because they never reached the model.

### Impact

- Tool authors who rely on `default`, numeric bounds, or string/array length/pattern constraints get no help from the model — the model can't honor a bound it never saw.
- Common workaround today is to duplicate every constraint into the param `description` string, which is redundant with the schema and easy to drift.
- Downstream harnesses that *evaluate* tool-call correctness can mis-score a model as producing "bad args" when the constraint was dropped in transit, not violated by the model.

### Suggested fix (two parts)

1. **Parse-level:** add the missing JSON-Schema keywords to `ToolProperty` (and `ToolFunctionParameters` where relevant) so they survive unmarshal. This is consistent with `enum`/`anyOf` already being retained.
2. **Presentation:** decide how the retained constraints are surfaced to the model — e.g. as annotations in the rendered type, or appended to the parameter description. This is a design choice I'd rather leave to maintainers; happy to open a PR once there's a preferred direction. (Part 1 alone is a prerequisite; without part 2 the fields survive parse but still won't reach the model.)

### OS / version

Ollama 0.31.1 (macOS). Struct unchanged on `main` at time of filing.

title: OpenAI compat: reasoning_effort "minimal" rejected with 400 instead of being accepted
author: wuisabel-gif
body:
### What is the issue?

The `/v1/chat/completions` OpenAI-compatible endpoint rejects
`reasoning_effort: "minimal"` with a 400 error, even though `"minimal"` is a
valid value in the OpenAI API (used by the GPT-5 series). Only `high`,
`medium`, `low`, `max`, and `none` are accepted.

This breaks OpenAI SDK clients that send `reasoning_effort="minimal"`: a
request that works against api.openai.com fails against Ollama with

    invalid reasoning value: 'minimal' (must be "high", "medium", "low", "max", or "none")

The rejection happens in the request translation layer, `openai/openai.go`
(`FromChatRequest`), where `effort` is validated against
`{high, medium, low, max, none}`. It returns the 400 before any inference
runs, so no model or GPU is involved. The `reasoning: {"effort": "minimal"}`
form fails identically, since both share the same code path.

Ollama has no distinct "minimal" think level, but `low` is the natural
equivalent (the least thinking without disabling it entirely), so `minimal`
should be normalized to `low` before that check rather than erroring.
Mapping it server-side (rather than telling users to send `low`) matters
because the value is often set by SDKs, agent frameworks, or shared configs
that target the OpenAI API generically — users of those clients can't rewrite
the field per-backend, so the request simply fails against Ollama.

This can be confirmed without a running server by adding a `minimal` case to
the existing `TestFromChatRequest_ReasoningEffort` table in
`openai/openai_test.go`:

    {name: "minimal maps to low", effort: effort("minimal"), want: "low"},

Against current code it fails:

    === RUN   TestFromChatRequest_ReasoningEffort/minimal_maps_to_low
        openai_test.go:92: unexpected error: invalid reasoning value: 'minimal' (must be "high", "medium", "low", "max", or "none")
    --- FAIL: TestFromChatRequest_ReasoningEffort/minimal_maps_to_low (0.00s)
    FAIL

Expected: `reasoning_effort: "minimal"` is accepted and treated as `low`,
matching OpenAI API compatibility.

### Relevant log output

```shell
invalid reasoning value: 'minimal' (must be "high", "medium", "low", "max", or "none")
```

### OS

macOS

### GPU

_No response_

### CPU

_No response_

### Ollama version

0.30.11

title: Windows: ollama run crashes with 0xc0000409 / CUDA error: shared object initialization failed on NVIDIA MX250 (Compute Capability 6.1) while official desktop app works
author: JRam1219
body: ### What is the issue? ## Environment - OS: Windows 11 Pro - Ollama: 0.31.2 - GPU: NVIDIA GeForce MX250 - Compute Capability: 6.1 - NVIDIA Driver: 582.66 - CUDA Version (reported by nvidia-smi): 13.0 ## Models tested - llama3.2:1b - qwen3.5:2b ## Problem Running either model from the CLI causes the llama-server process to terminate. Example: ```bash ollama run llama3.2:1b ``` returns: ```text Error: 500 Internal Server Error: llama-server process has terminated: exit status 0xc0000409: CUDA error: shared object initialization failed ``` The same occurs with `qwen3.5:2b`. ## Expected behavior The CLI should behave the same as the official desktop application and successfully start inference. ## Actual behavior The desktop application works correctly (including completely offline) and successfully runs both models. The CLI consistently crashes with the error above. ## Additional observations The issue became noticeable after updating the NVIDIA driver. Prior to the update, the NVIDIA GPU appeared inactive and inference seemed to run on the CPU rather than CUDA. Before updating the driver, the NVIDIA GPU was effectively inactive and inference appeared to run on the CPU (slow but functional). After updating the driver: - the desktop application successfully uses the GPU, - but every CLI inference crashes. ## Relevant server log The server first reports: ```text skipping CUDA device — compute capability not in compiled architectures ``` for the MX250 (Compute Capability 6.1), but shortly afterward reports: ```text inference compute library=CUDA compute=6.1 ``` before the llama-server process terminates. This appears contradictory because the GPU is first rejected and then selected. ## Additional information - `ollama list` works. - `ollama show` works. - `/api/tags` works. - Ollama installation appears complete (`cuda_v12`, `cuda_v13`, `llama-server.exe`, DLLs present). [app.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29930275/app.log) [server.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29930304/server.log) ### Relevant log output ```shell time=2026-07-11T06:23:44.008+08:00 level=INFO source=runner.go:60 msg="discovering available GPUs..." time=2026-07-11T06:23:48.270+08:00 level=INFO source=llama_server.go:299 msg="skipping CUDA device — compute capability not in compiled architectures" device="NVIDIA GeForce MX250" cc=610 archs="[750 800 860 890 1000 1200]" time=2026-07-11T06:23:53.489+08:00 level=INFO source=types.go:32 msg="inference compute" id=0 library=CUDA compute=6.1 name=CUDA0 description="NVIDIA GeForce MX250" driver=13.0 total="2.0 GiB" available="1.6 GiB" Error: 500 Internal Server Error: llama-server process has terminated: exit status 0xc0000409: The system detected an overrun of a stack-based buffer in this application. CUDA error: shared object initialization failed ``` ### OS Windows ### GPU Nvidia ### CPU Intel ### Ollama version 0.31.2

title: Mixed NVIDIA/AMD systems: GPU-selection env vars don't affect scheduling
author: zardust
body:
### What is the issue?

On a hybrid laptop with both an NVIDIA dGPU (CUDA) and an AMD iGPU (ROCm), the model scheduler selects the AMD iGPU even when `ROCR_VISIBLE_DEVICES=-1` is set and confirmed present in the server's own config dump. Per the docs, this value should "ignore the GPUs and force CPU usage" for the ROCm backend — in a mixed-backend system, I'd expect that to mean ROCm reports no usable device and the still-healthy CUDA device gets used instead. Instead, the ROCm device continues to show up in `inference compute` and continues to get scheduled for inference. Only setting `OLLAMA_LLM_LIBRARY=cuda_v13` (forcing the backend directly, bypassing discovery/scheduling entirely) reliably pins inference to the NVIDIA GPU.

## Environment

- Ollama version: 0.31.2
- OS: Windows 11 (German), Version 25H2 (Build 26200.8655)
- Laptop: ASUS ProArt P16
- CPU/iGPU: AMD Ryzen AI 9 370 (Radeon 890M Graphics, gfx1150)
- dGPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 Laptop GPU, driver 13.3 (CUDA compute 12.0)
- Both GPUs report ~24GB available (890M via shared system RAM, RTX 5090 via dedicated VRAM)

## Steps to reproduce

1. Have a system with both an NVIDIA CUDA-capable GPU and a ROCm-capable AMD iGPU installed and both healthy/detected (confirmed via `inference compute` log lines for both).
2. Set `ROCR_VISIBLE_DEVICES=-1` as a user environment variable. Fully quit the Ollama tray app and relaunch (not just killing `ollama.exe serve`, since the tray app respawns it).
3. Load a small model that fits comfortably on either GPU (e.g. `ollama run llama3.2:1b`).
4. Observe `ollama ps` / Task Manager — the model still runs on the AMD iGPU.
5. Only setting `OLLAMA_LLM_LIBRARY=cuda_v13` forces the NVIDIA GPU to be used instead.

## Expected behavior

Per `docs/gpu.mdx`: *"If you want to ignore the GPUs and force CPU usage, use an invalid GPU ID (e.g., '-1')."* On a system where ROCm is the only backend, I'd expect `-1` to force CPU. On a mixed-backend system where CUDA is also present and healthy, I'd expect the ROCm device to at least be excluded from the scheduler's candidate list so the healthy CUDA device is used, rather than the ROCm device continuing to be selected as if the variable had no effect at all.

## Actual behavior

The variable is read into the server config correctly (confirmed in `server.log`'s `"server config" env="map[...]"` line) but has no visible effect on which device the scheduler picks. The AMD iGPU (`ROCm0`) continues to be selected over the NVIDIA dGPU (`CUDA0`) for models that fit on either.

## Relevant logs

Startup — both devices detected:
```
source=types.go:32 msg="inference compute" id=0 filter_id=0 library=ROCm compute=gfx1150 name=ROCm0 description="AMD Radeon(TM) 890M Graphics" libdirs=ollama,rocm_v7_1 driver=0.0 pci_id="" type=discrete total="24.2 GiB" available="24.0 GiB"
source=types.go:32 msg="inference compute" id=0 filter_id=0 library=CUDA compute=12.0 name=CUDA0 description="NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 Laptop GPU" libdirs=ollama,cuda_v13 driver=13.3 pci_id=0000:65:00.0 type=discrete total="23.9 GiB" available="22.6 GiB"
```

Server config with `ROCR_VISIBLE_DEVICES=-1` set — variable present, no effect on scheduling:
```
time=2026-07-11T17:26:52.003+02:00 level=INFO source=routes.go:1947 msg="server config" env="map[CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: GGML_VK_VISIBLE_DEVICES: GPU_DEVICE_ORDINAL: HIP_VISIBLE_DEVICES: HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION: HTTPS_PROXY: HTTP_PROXY: LLAMA_ARG_FIT: LLAMA_ARG_FIT_TARGET: NO_PROXY: OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH:0 OLLAMA_DEBUG:INFO OLLAMA_DEBUG_LOG_REQUESTS:false OLLAMA_EDITOR: OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION:false OLLAMA_GO_TEMPLATE:true OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD:0 OLLAMA_HOST:http://127.0.0.1:11434 OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE:5m0s OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE: OLLAMA_LLM_LIBRARY: OLLAMA_LOAD_TIMEOUT:5m0s OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS:0 OLLAMA_MAX_QUEUE:512 OLLAMA_MAX_TRANSFER_STREAMS:4 OLLAMA_MODELS:C:\Users\eugen\.ollama\models OLLAMA_NOHISTORY:false OLLAMA_NOPRUNE:false OLLAMA_NO_CLOUD:false OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL:1 OLLAMA_ORIGINS:[...] OLLAMA_REMOTES:[ollama.com] OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD:false OLLAMA_VULKAN:true ROCR_VISIBLE_DEVICES:-1]"
```

## Workaround

Setting `OLLAMA_LLM_LIBRARY=cuda_v13` (documented under "Experimental LLM Library Override" in `docs/troubleshooting.md`) forces the CUDA backend directly and works reliably, but this bypasses backend discovery/fallback entirely rather than just excluding the unwanted device, and requires knowing the exact internal library name (`cuda_v13`) rather than relying on the documented device-visibility variable.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### OS

_No response_

### GPU

_No response_

### CPU

_No response_

### Ollama version

_No response_

title: Please allow thread number setting globally for all models and interfaces at the service level.
author: Martyn575
body:
# Background:

I have a radxa orion o6 and like a lot of ARM64 systems, it has efficiency cores: 

```
root@orion-o6:/opt# lscpu -e
CPU NODE SOCKET CORE ONLINE    MAXMHZ   MINMHZ       MHZ
  0    0      0    0    yes 2600.1980 799.8650  799.8650
  1    0      0    0    yes 1799.9980 799.9990 1799.9980
  2    0      0    1    yes 1799.9980 799.9990 1799.9980
  3    0      0    2    yes 1799.9980 799.9990 1799.9980
  4    0      0    3    yes 1799.9980 799.9990 1799.9980
  5    0      0    0    yes 2299.9089 799.8580  799.8580
  6    0      0    1    yes 2299.9089 799.8580  799.8580
  7    0      0    2    yes 2199.8779 799.8400  799.8400
  8    0      0    3    yes 2199.8779 799.8400  799.8400
  9    0      0    4    yes 2499.8970 799.8650  799.8650
 10    0      0    5    yes 2499.8970 799.8650  799.8650
 11    0      0    6    yes 2600.1980 799.8650  799.8650
```

So i find that if i edit the systemctl edit ollama.service and insert the line:

`AllowedCPUs=0,5-11`

And i send down `num_threads=8` down to ollama backend through my own client, then i'm able to get a 12-13% performance increase as well as using less electricity.

I would like to set this option system wide for all models because everything i've tested on, it's faster this way for my hardware. 

# And this is where i run into problems: 

Other clients don't send down the num_threads to ollama, and so because they don't, ollama tries to run 12 threads worth of work on 8 cpus and the performance is unusable for those other clients. If i run Ellama, it wont work. If i use Odysseus, it wont work. 

For some reason if i try and set `Environment="OLLAMA_NUM_THREAD=8"` in the service file, it's just ignored and ollama starts up with detected 12 threads trying to funnel all 12 processes into 8 cores which slows performance down from seconds to many many minutes. 

I have also tried: `export OLLAMA_NUM_THREAD=8` on the bash commandline before starting ollama, that is ignored too. 

There are loads of third party interfaces that use ollama, it's impractical to rewrite all of those interfaces to insert num_threads. Third party interfaces should not have to adapt to hardware differences. Ollama will assume 12, and try and push 12 workloads into 8 cpus, and the result will be devastating on performance and cause ollama to be essentially frozen / locked up for a long period of time until ollama is restarted. So you either can't use other interfaces, or you can't optimise either which is an bizaar situation to be in. 

It's also impractical to fork off new models every time they are downloaded and force in num_threads into the model itself. 

It's dangerous to have half of the solution on the backend and half on the client end. Both halfs of the solution NEED be on the backend. 

# What would be super helpful to have: 

There needs to be a way to explicitly set the default number of threads that are used by ollama for all models, so that interfaces and models can be completely agnostic of the hardware. 

e.g.: 

`Environment="OLLAMA_NUM_THREAD=8"`

Really needs to set the number of threads used. And this can be used in conjunction with AllowedCPUs. 

This is because many ARM64 systems have efficiency cores which if they are enabled not only will use more energy but they will also slow down ollama substantially. 

If the client THEN wishes to increase or decrease these threads then they can, but having the option to specify the default threads used by ollama would be a huge benefit and will allow many interfaces to just "work out of the box" with tuned ollama settings and will stop performance tweeking from breaking with different clients.

title: `ollama launch claude -- --channels` ignores channels with "Channels are not currently available"
author: oming
body:
### What is the issue?

## Description

When launching Claude Code through `ollama launch claude`, the `--channels` option does not work.

Following the official Ollama documentation for the Telegram integration:

https://docs.ollama.com/integrations/claude-code#telegram

the Telegram channel is configured successfully, but `ollama launch claude -- --channels ...` always reports:

> --channels ignored (plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official)
> Channels are not currently available

However, running Claude Code directly with the same environment variables works correctly.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Install the Telegram plugin following the Claude Code documentation:

   https://code.claude.com/docs/ko/channels#supported-channels

2. Restart Claude Code with channels enabled:

   ```bash
   ollama launch claude -- --channels plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official
   ```

3. Claude starts, but the following message is shown:

   ```
   --channels ignored (plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official)

   Channels are not currently available
   ```

4. Running `/status` shows:

   ```
   Channels: Configured but not active (not currently available): plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official
   ```

5. `/mcp` initially showed the Telegram MCP server disconnected. After reconnecting the plugin and completing the pairing process, the MCP server remains connected, but the issue persists.

## Actual behavior

`ollama launch claude` always reports:

```text
--channels ignored (plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official)

Channels are not currently available
```

`/status` shows:

```text
Version:             2.1.207
Session ID:          xxxxxxxx
Channels:            Configured but not active (not currently available): plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official
cwd:                 /Users/xxxxx
Auth token:          ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN
Anthropic base URL:  http://127.0.0.1:11434

Model:               ornith:35b-claude
MCP servers:         1 connected · /mcp
Setting sources:     User settings
```

Although the MCP server is connected, Channels never become active.

## Expected behavior

The Telegram channel should become active and start listening for messages, just like when Claude Code is launched directly.

## Works correctly when launching Claude directly

The following command works as expected:

```bash
ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=ollama \
ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:11434 \
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="" \
claude --channels plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official
```

Output:

```text
Channels (experimental) messages from plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official inject directly in this session · restart without --channels to stop
```

`/status`:

```text
Version:             2.1.207
Session ID:          xxxxxxxx
Channels:            Listening for messages from plugin:telegram@claude-plugins-official
cwd:                 /Users/xxxxx
Auth token:          ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN
Anthropic base URL:  http://localhost:11434

Model:               ornith:35b-claude
MCP servers:         1 connected · /mcp
Setting sources:     User settings
```

## Environment

- Claude Code: 2.1.207
- telegram @ claude-plugins-official Version: 0.0.6

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### OS

macOS

### GPU

Apple

### CPU

Apple

### Ollama version

0.31.2

title: Streaming handlers (generate/chat/push) leak goroutines and runner semaphore slots on client disconnect
author: ErenAta16
body:
### What happened

`GenerateHandler`, `PushHandler`, `ChatHandler`, and `handleNativeChat` all stream results to the client through an unbuffered channel with a bare blocking send, e.g. in `GenerateHandler` (`server/routes.go`):

```go
ch := make(chan any)
go func() {
    defer close(ch)
    if err := r.Completion(c.Request.Context(), llm.CompletionRequest{...}, func(cr llm.CompletionResponse) {
        ...
        ch <- res   // blocking send, no ctx check
    }); err != nil {
        ...
        ch <- gin.H{"error": err.Error()}   // blocking send, no ctx check
    }
}()
...
streamResponse(c, ch)
```

`streamResponse` hands `ch` to `gin.Context.Stream`, whose implementation is:

```go
func (c *Context) Stream(step func(w io.Writer) bool) bool {
	w := c.Writer
	clientGone := w.CloseNotify()
	for {
		select {
		case <-clientGone:
			return true
		default:
			keepOpen := step(w)
			...
```

When the client disconnects (closed tab, killed `curl`, proxy timeout, etc.), `Stream` returns via the `clientGone` case without calling `step` again, so nothing ever reads from `ch` after that point. The producer goroutine is still running `r.Completion`, and the very next time its callback tries `ch <- res`, it blocks forever, since the channel is unbuffered and has no reader. The goroutine leaks permanently, and because `r.Completion` never returns, `defer s.sem.Release(1)` in `llamaServerRunner.Completion` (`llm/llama_server.go`) never runs either, so the runner's concurrency semaphore slot stays held for the rest of the process's life. Enough disconnected streaming requests landing at the wrong moment will exhaust a runner's concurrency and start queuing or rejecting new requests that should otherwise succeed.

This reproduces reliably: start a `/api/generate` or `/api/chat` stream, close the connection mid-generation (e.g. `curl --max-time 0.5`), and the corresponding goroutine (visible via `runtime.NumGoroutine()` / `net/http/pprof`) never terminates for that request.

### Why I'm filing a new issue instead of piling onto an existing one

I found #15876, which is an open PR that fixes exactly this bug shape for `PullHandler`:

```go
func sendStreamValue(ctx context.Context, ch chan<- any, v any) bool {
	select {
	case ch <- v:
		return true
	case <-ctx.Done():
		return false
	}
}
```

used as `if !sendStreamValue(ctx, ch, r) { cancel() }` in place of the bare `ch <- r`. That confirms the team has already identified and has the right fix pattern for this class of bug. But the PR's diff to `server/routes.go` only touches `PullHandler` — `GenerateHandler`, `PushHandler`, `ChatHandler`, and `handleNativeChat` still use the unprotected `ch <- ...` form on current `main`, and none of them are touched by #15876. Since `/api/generate` and `/api/chat` are the most heavily used streaming endpoints, this seemed worth tracking on its own rather than assuming it'll be picked up as a side effect of the Pull-specific fix.

### Suggested fix

Apply the same `sendStreamValue`-style guarded send (or an equivalent `select` with `ctx.Done()`) to the `ch <-` calls in `GenerateHandler`, `PushHandler`, `ChatHandler`, and `handleNativeChat`, the same way #15876 does for `PullHandler`. If `sendStreamValue` lands as an unexported helper in `server/routes.go`, it would cover all of these call sites without needing a bespoke fix per handler.

### Version

Reproduced against current `main`.

title: Anthropic-compat /v1/messages silently ignores cache_control; usage missing cache_creation/cache_read_input_tokens
author: peter20201011-cmyk
body:
## Summary

Ollama's Anthropic-compatible `/v1/messages` endpoint accepts `cache_control: {type: "ephemeral"}` in system/content blocks **without error**, but does not implement Anthropic Prompt Cache semantics: it stores nothing, and the `usage` object never returns `cache_creation_input_tokens` or `cache_read_input_tokens`. On the second request with an identical prefix, `input_tokens` still counts the full prefix — i.e. zero cache hits.

Clients that rely on Anthropic prompt caching (e.g. Claude Code pointed at Ollama via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:11434`) therefore show 0 cache and re-process the full system prompt + history every turn, with the latency and cost that implies.

## Environment

- Ollama: 0.31.1
- macOS (Apple Silicon, arm64)
- Model: `glm-5.2:cloud` (Ollama Cloud)
- Endpoint: `POST http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1/messages` (Anthropic-compat)

## Reproduction

Two requests with an identical, sufficiently long system prefix and `cache_control: ephemeral`, differing only in the user message:

```bash
TOKEN="$ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN"
SYS='你是一个测试助手，只回复简短中文。固定前缀填充：AAAA0001 BBBB0002 CCCC0003 DDDD0004 EEEE0005 FFFF0006 GGGG0007 HHHH0008 IIII0009 JJJJ0010 KKKK0011 LLLL0012 MMMM0013 NNNN0014 OOOO0015 PPPP0016 QQQQ0017 RRRR0018 SSSS0019 TTTT0020 UUUU0021 VVVV0022 WWWW0023 XXXX0024 YYYY0025 ZZZZ0026 AAAA0027 BBBB0028 CCCC0029 DDDD0030。'

# Request 1 — prime the cache
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1/messages \
  -H "x-api-key: $TOKEN" -H "authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" -H "content-type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"glm-5.2:cloud","max_tokens":30,
       "system":[{"type":"text","text":"'"$SYS"'","cache_control":{"type":"ephemeral"}}],
       "messages":[{"role":"user","content":"reply: 1"}]}'
# → HTTP 200, usage: {"input_tokens": 165, "output_tokens": 30}

# Request 2 — same prefix + same cache_control, different user msg
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1/messages \
  -H "x-api-key: $TOKEN" -H "authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" -H "content-type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"glm-5.2:cloud","max_tokens":30,
       "system":[{"type":"text","text":"'"$SYS"'","cache_control":{"type":"ephemeral"}}],
       "messages":[{"role":"user","content":"reply: 2"}]}'
# → HTTP 200, usage: {"input_tokens": 165, "output_tokens": 30}
```

## Actual behavior

```
usage1: {"input_tokens": 165, "output_tokens": 30}
usage2: {"input_tokens": 165, "output_tokens": 30}
```

- `cache_control` is **silently accepted** (HTTP 200, no error, no warning).
- Request 1 `usage` has **no** `cache_creation_input_tokens`.
- Request 2 `usage` has **no** `cache_read_input_tokens`, and `input_tokens` is still the full 165 — the prefix is not served from any cache.

## Expected behavior (per Anthropic Messages API prompt caching)

- Request 1 `usage` should include `cache_creation_input_tokens` (tokens written to cache) and `input_tokens` reflecting only the non-cached portion.
- Request 2 `usage` should include `cache_read_input_tokens` (tokens served from cache), with `input_tokens` reflecting only the non-cached delta.

Reference: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching

## Impact

Agentic clients that target the Anthropic Messages API and depend on prompt caching get no benefit when routed through Ollama:

- Claude Code with `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:11434` reports 0 cache tokens every turn.
- The full system prompt + accumulated history is re-processed on every turn, inflating latency and token usage — most visible in long sessions (e.g. multi-step browser-scraping / eval workflows).

## Ask

Either:

1. Implement Anthropic prompt-cache semantics on `/v1/messages`: store the keyed prefix, and return `cache_creation_input_tokens` / `cache_read_input_tokens` in `usage` so clients can benefit; or
2. If intentionally unsupported, reject or warn on `cache_control` with a clear message and document that the Anthropic-compat endpoint does not support prompt caching — so clients can fall back gracefully instead of silently losing the cache.

## Related

- #16714 requests provider-cache passthrough on Ollama Cloud (cloud-layer savings). This issue is distinct: it is about the **Anthropic-compat `/v1/messages` endpoint protocol fields** (`cache_control` handling + `cache_*_input_tokens` in `usage`) being absent, which affects any client using the Anthropic Messages API shape regardless of cloud provider caching. They overlap in spirit but this one is reproducible at the endpoint level with a minimal curl.

title: Ollama Cloud: expose embedding and reranker models (Pro plan)
author: qwexs
body:
# Ollama Cloud: expose embedding and reranker models (Pro plan)

**TL;DR** — As a Pro subscriber using Ollama Cloud for chat via `OLLAMA_API_KEY`, I'd like embedding and reranker models exposed on the cloud endpoint too. Local Ollama has partial coverage (see #10989, #16076), but Ollama Cloud currently looks chat-only. There's a related cloud-model-request thread (#17100) that doesn't mention embedding/reranker — surfacing it here.

## What I observe today

- Cloud `GET /api/tags` lists only chat/instruct models.
- No `/v1/embeddings` route for cloud-hosted models (works fine on a local Ollama instance).
- No rerank endpoint (`/api/rerank` or otherwise) exposed on the cloud.

## What I'd like to consume (billed through the same Pro plan)

- **Text embeddings** via `/v1/embeddings`:
  - `nomic-embed-text`
  - `mxbai-embed-large`
  - `qwen3-embedding` (the GGUF build that already exists in the local library)
- **Rerankers** via a `/v1/rerank`-style endpoint (or whatever shape Ollama prefers):
  - `bge-reranker-v2-m3`
  - `qwen3-reranker`

## Why this matters — use case

Local-first RAG pipeline: BM25 + vector search + cross-encoder rerank. Today both the embedding model (`nomic-embed-text`) and the reranker (`bge-reranker-v2-m3`) run on a desktop GPU. If these were available on Ollama Cloud, I could drop the local reranker entirely and unify billing / observability / auth under one provider.

## Community demand on the local side (for context)

- **#3368** — Reranking models (open since Mar 2024, 102 comments, several stalled PRs).
- **#10989** — Support for qwen3-embedding and qwen3-reranker (open since Jun 2025).
- **#16076** — First-class embedding & reranker support on the MLX path (open since May 2026).
- **#17100** — New model requests for Ollama Cloud (open, currently lists LLM models only).

These threads show the demand; none of them is specifically scoped to Ollama Cloud, which is why I'm filing this separately.

## Questions

1. Are embedding models on the Ollama Cloud roadmap? If yes — rough timeline and which model families (nomic, mxbai, bge, qwen3-embedding)?
2. Same question for rerankers — or is the cloud intentionally chat-only for now?
3. Is the limitation technical (KV-cache / VRAM shape, GPU scheduling, model-server isolation) or a product-scope decision? Knowing which would help me plan around it.
4. Today, is pointing `/v1/embeddings` at a local Ollama instance while chat stays on the cloud an officially supported setup, or is there a better workaround?

Happy to test a preview, share more about the use case, or split this into separate issues (one for embedding, one for rerank) if that's easier to triage.

Thanks,
Sergey (Pro subscriber)

title: qwen35moe decode throughput regressed ~23% at v0.30.9 on RTX 3090 (bisected to 5 commits, prime suspect: llama.cpp b9637 bump)
author: C-LaForest
body:
## Summary

Bisected a warm-decode throughput regression on `qwen3.6:35b` (family `qwen35moe`, MoE-A3B, Q4_K_M) that landed in Ollama v0.30.9 and has persisted through v0.31.1. Cold tok/s at 98K ctx dropped from ~155 → ~120 (-23%) with no VRAM or spill change. Reproduced across 10 discrete Docker Hub tags with the step function isolated to v0.30.8 → v0.30.9. This is separate from #17099's v0.31.2 memory-estimate inflation — that one is a spill / VRAM issue; this one is pure decode kernel throughput.

## Environment

- NVIDIA RTX 3090 24 GB (Ampere, SM_8.6 / compute cap 8.6)
- Linux, podman container (`ollama/ollama:<tag>`)
- Model: `qwen3.6:35b` (family `qwen35moe`, 36B total / 3B active per token, Q4_K_M)
- Context: 98304
- Env: `OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1`, `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0`, `OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1`, `OLLAMA_NEW_ENGINE=true`
- Driver: 610.43.02 (unchanged across the sweep)

## Reproduction — bisect table

Ran an identical benchmark harness (single-request `/api/generate`, `num_predict=50`, `num_ctx=98304`, force-evict between context points, cold prompt + warm second call at each tag) across every semver Docker Hub tag from v0.30.4 through v0.31.1:

| Tag | cold tok/s | warm tok/s | GPU % | VRAM_GB | CPU RAM_GB | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.30.4 | 155.1 | 149.5 | 96.3 | 23.71 | 0.92 | good |
| 0.30.5 | 155.8 | 153.4 | 96.3 | 23.71 | 0.92 | good |
| 0.30.6 | 155.3 | 153.1 | 96.3 | 23.71 | 0.92 | good |
| 0.30.7 | 156.2 | 154.0 | 96.3 | 23.71 | 0.92 | good |
| **0.30.8** | **155.9** | **154.7** | **96.3** | **23.71** | **0.92** | **LAST GOOD** |
| **0.30.9** | **114.0** | **97.1** | **96.1** | **23.70** | **0.96** | **FIRST BAD** — cold-load time also jumped 6.46→10.48s |
| 0.30.10 | 120.4 | 94.8 | 96.1 | 23.70 | 0.96 | still bad; cold-load still ~10s |
| 0.30.11 | 117.9 | 116.0 | 96.1 | 23.70 | 0.96 | cold-load recovers to 5.70s, decode still ~120 |
| 0.31.0 | 120.5 | 85.0 | 96.1 | 23.70 | 0.96 | still ~120 plateau |
| 0.31.1 | 120.4 | 118.6 | 96.1 | 23.70 | 0.96 | still ~120 plateau |

Delta at 0.30.8 → 0.30.9:
- **Cold decode: 155.9 → 114.0 tok/s = -27%**
- **Warm decode: 154.7 → 97.1 tok/s = -37%** (warm samples on 50-tok predictions are jittery; the 0.31.0 warm 85.0 vs 0.31.1 warm 118.6 shows the jitter — cold is the cleaner signal)
- **CPU RAM: 0.92 → 0.96 GB (+40 MB, negligible)**
- **VRAM: 23.71 → 23.70 GB (-10 MB, negligible)**
- **Cold-load time: 6.46 → 10.48s (+62%)** — this partially recovers by v0.30.11

Cold tok/s stabilizes at a ~120 plateau from v0.30.9 through v0.31.1 — the regression persisted for 5 minor versions and is still present in the newest release.

## Commit range (verified via `gh api repos/ollama/ollama/compare/v0.30.8...v0.30.9`)

Only 5 commits in the delta:

| # | SHA | Title | Suspect? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **7ea692c** | llama: update llama.cpp to b9637 (#16609) | ⭐ Most likely — kernel selection heuristics in llama.cpp affect matmul throughput |
| 2 | 993acc7 | model: update lfm2 parser/renderer for optional thinking (#16359) | Unrelated to our model family |
| 3 | bbb40a0 | server: context shift for context windows larger than 8k (#16712) | Plausible — we run at 98K, above the 8K threshold this change affects |
| 4 | 0f047fe | llm: context shift allow shiftable prompts (#16764) | Plausible — companion to #3 |
| 5 | 9e4ed74 | integration: look for the "hf" tool in integration tests (#16765) | Unrelated (test tooling) |

Prime hypothesis: **7ea692c** (llama.cpp bump to b9637) retuned matmul kernel selection on Ampere / SM_8.6 and routed qwen35moe's dominant quantized matmul to a slower kernel path. Consistent with:
- No VRAM change (same tensor sizes, same memory layout)
- Throughput drop applies universally to cold and warm (kernel-selection is per-op, not per-batch)
- Simultaneous cold-load slowdown (extra kernel-selection at graph-compile time)
- Recovery of cold-load in v0.30.11 without decode recovery (a separate load-path fix, not the kernel issue)

Secondary hypothesis: bbb40a0 + 0f047fe changed context-shift routing at ctx > 8K. Our test is at 98K, well into that path.

Disambiguating between the two hypotheses would require an llama.cpp-level bisect (b9636 vs b9637) which requires building from source — I haven't done that.

## Not affected

- Memory estimation: identical VRAM/RAM split across the sweep (except the negligible ~10-40 MB shifts).
- Model compatibility: model loads and responds correctly on all tags; no output-quality regression observed.
- OLLAMA_NEW_ENGINE toggle: tested `false` on v0.31.2 (different regression) — no effect. Have not tested `false` on the 0.30.9+ range but the code path suggested by the commits above is not in the engine switching logic.

## Reference: separate acute regression at v0.31.2

The v0.31.2 memory-estimator inflation is tracked in #17099. That one adds ~1.88 GB CPU spill on top of this decode regression — different mechanism, different fix locus. The two regressions compound but are independent. Comment thread there has additional context: [comment 1](https://github.com/ollama/ollama/issues/17099#issuecomment-4936782350), [comment 2](https://github.com/ollama/ollama/issues/17099#issuecomment-4938305754).

## Willing to gather more data

Happy to:
- Run `OLLAMA_DEBUG=1` on v0.30.8 vs v0.30.9 to capture kernel-selection decisions
- Bisect llama.cpp itself if someone points at the b9636…b9637 commit range
- Test with `bbb40a0` reverted (if maintainer prefers to rule out the context-shift path) — would require a custom build
- Re-run the perf grid at other ctx points (32K, 64K, 128K) to show whether the regression is ctx-dependent
